summary_text:
S:
The patient reports difficulty swallowing over a period of time, with pain when swallowing certain foods. He notes that the difficulty is exacerbated by larger food pieces. He has experienced some weight gain over the past month and reports feeling stressed due to a recent move. He also mentions a stressful period in his life, leading to increased consumption of pizza and burgers. He reports his dog's travel is a source of stress and worries about the dog's well-being during air travel, specifically regarding cargo hold issues.

O:
Blood pressure is 130/77 mmHg. Heart rate is 72 bpm. Oxygen saturation is 100%. Respiratory rate is 19 breaths per minute. Temperature is 98.6°F. Physical exam reveals no rash, lesions, or tenderness to palpation in the neck. Abdomen is nondistended with active bowel sounds. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. Neurological exam is unremarkable. An upper endoscopy is scheduled.

A:
Cute esophagitis is suspected based on the patient's reported symptoms and barium swallow findings. The patient's weight gain is a contributing factor to his symptoms. The stress and recent move may be exacerbating the esophageal issues.

P:
Rescribe protonix 40mg once daily. Prescribe carafate 1g four times daily for one month. Schedule an upper endoscopy for follow-up. Encourage the patient to change their diet, decreasing alcohol and caffeine intake, especially once settled in his new location. Advise the patient to eat slowly and chew food thoroughly. Advise against citrus foods, fruits, and spicy foods until symptoms improve. Schedule a follow-up appointment in one week.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Difficulty swallowing.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Raymond Taylor is a pleasant 67-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for difficulty swallowing. The patient notes that the pain has been occurring for the last several weeks. The pain radiates to his chest when he swallows. He notes that he does not have pain every time he eats but mostly when he has big pieces of food as they seem to get stuck. Mr. Taylor notes that it has been stressful for him the past couple of months as they moved from the West Coast to the East Coast, so he has been drinking more, and having pizza, and burgers more. He denies any weight loss, but endorses weight gain. The patient states that he has epigastric pain, but denies dark, tarry stools.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports weight gain.
HENT: Reports dysphagia.
Gastrointestinal: Reports epigastric pain. Denies dark, tarry stools.
Neurological: Positive stress.

VITALS

BP: 133/70.
Heart rate looks good.
Temperature is within normal limits.
SpO2: 100%.
Respiratory rate: 19.

PHYSICAL EXAM

EYES: Equal and reactive to light.
NECK: No adenopathy, thyromegaly.
RESPIRATORY: Normal respiratory effort no respiratory distress
GI/GU: Non-distended Active bowel sounds. Pain to palpation of epigastric area. Negative McMurphy's Sign. No peritoneal signs. No rebound.
SKIN: No rash, no lesion, no bruising.
MSK: Examination of the mouth reveals no obvious ulcers or evidence of thrush present. Tonsils are midline of the neck.

RESULTS

The barium swallow study revealed two areas of mild narrowing in the mid and lower portions of your esophagus.

ASSESSMENT

Acute esophagitis.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and barium swallow findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have prescribed the patient Protonix 40 mg first thing in the morning to treat his acute esophagitis. I have also prescribed the patient Carafate 1 gram 4 times daily for 1 month to help coat the lining of his esophagus and stomach. I recommended a upper endoscopy for further evaluation. I have also advised him to change his diet, decrease alcohol, and caffeine. I have also advised him to avoid citrus foods, fruits, and spicy foods until his symptoms have improved. We discussed that he should eat slowly and chew his food thoroughly to avoid big pieces of food. All questions were answered.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 1 week for his endoscopy.

section_miscategorizations.reason: Scheduling an upper endoscopy is a management action/plan item, not an already-performed procedure or observed finding.

section_miscategorizations.text: Cute esophagitis is suspected based on the patient's reported symptoms and barium swallow findings.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This sentence references barium swallow findings, which are objective test results and belong in the Objective section; the diagnostic impression of esophagitis itself is appropriate for Assessment, but the explicit mention of test findings is miscategorized.

section_miscategorizations.text: Schedule an upper endoscopy for follow-up.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is correctly a plan item, but it duplicates the earlier miscategorized statement in O; organizationally it would be clearer to keep the scheduling action only in the Plan section.
